variants.title: Level 1: Understanding Classes (Student Class)
variants.description: Learn what a class is by creating a simple Student class with basic properties.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: A class contains fields (variables) that describe an object. Think about what information a student would have.

variants.title: Level 1: Understanding Classes (Book Class)
variants.description: Create a Book class to understand the concept of classes as blueprints.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Classes are like blueprints. A Book blueprint should define what properties every book has.

variants.title: Level 2: Creating Objects (Student Instance)
variants.description: Learn how to create objects (instances) from a class.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Objects are created using the 'new' keyword. Think of it as creating a real student from the Student blueprint.

variants.title: Level 2: Creating Objects (Car Instance)
variants.description: Practice creating objects by making a Car instance.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use the 'new' keyword to create an instance of the Car class. Each object is a separate entity.

variants.title: Level 3: Setting Object Properties (Student Data)
variants.description: Learn how to assign values to object fields after creation.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use the dot notation (.) to access object properties. Objects can hold different values even from the same class.

variants.title: Level 3: Setting Object Properties (Product Data)
variants.description: Practice setting object properties with a Product class.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Each object instance can store its own unique data. Access properties using objectName.propertyName

variants.title: Level 4: Multiple Objects (Student Comparison)
variants.description: Create multiple objects to understand that each object is independent.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Each object is independent. Creating multiple objects shows how the same class can produce different instances.

variants.title: Level 4: Multiple Objects (Animal Collection)
variants.description: Create multiple Animal objects to see object independence.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Think about how different animals can be created from the same Animal blueprint but have different characteristics.

variants.title: Level 5: Basic Methods (Student Display)
variants.description: Add a simple method to a class to introduce behavior.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Methods define what objects can do. A simple method might just print information about the object.

variants.title: Level 5: Basic Methods (Calculator Function)
variants.description: Create a method that performs a simple calculation.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Methods can return values. Think about what type of value an 'add' method should return.

variants.title: Level 1: Class Design (Employee Management)
variants.description: Design a more complex class with multiple data types and understand encapsulation basics.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use private fields to protect data and public methods to provide controlled access.

variants.title: Level 1: Class Design (Bank Account)
variants.description: Create a BankAccount class that demonstrates data protection principles.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Financial data should be private for security. Provide safe ways to access the information.

variants.title: Level 2: Constructors (Person Initialization)
variants.description: Learn about constructors for proper object initialization.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Constructors ensure objects are properly initialized when created. They have the same name as the class.

variants.title: Level 2: Constructors (Rectangle Setup)
variants.description: Create a Rectangle class with constructor for geometric calculations.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Constructors can take parameters to set initial values. Methods can perform calculations using the object's data.

variants.title: Level 3: Method Overloading (Calculator)
variants.description: Implement method overloading to understand polymorphism basics.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Method overloading allows multiple methods with the same name but different parameters.

variants.title: Level 3: Method Overloading (String Processor)
variants.description: Create overloaded methods for string processing.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Different parameter combinations allow the same method name to perform related but different operations.

variants.title: Level 4: Static Members (Math Utilities)
variants.description: Understand static methods and fields that belong to the class, not instances.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Static members belong to the class itself, not to any specific instance. They can be accessed without creating objects.

variants.title: Level 4: Static Members (Counter Class)
variants.description: Create a class that tracks how many instances have been created.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Static fields are shared across all instances. Use them to track class-wide information.

variants.title: Level 5: Object Relationships (Student-Course)
variants.description: Model relationships between objects using composition.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Objects can contain other objects as fields. This models real-world relationships like students taking courses.

variants.title: Level 5: Object Relationships (Library System)
variants.description: Create a simple library system with books and library classes.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Model real-world systems by having objects interact with each other through methods.

variants.title: Level 1: Advanced Design Patterns (Singleton)
variants.description: Implement the Singleton pattern to ensure only one instance exists.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Singleton ensures only one instance exists. Use private constructor and static method for controlled access.

variants.title: Level 1: Advanced Design Patterns (Factory)
variants.description: Create a factory pattern for object creation based on type.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Factory pattern encapsulates object creation logic. Use conditional statements to return appropriate instances.

variants.title: Level 2: Generic Classes (Container)
variants.description: Create a generic container class that can hold any type of object.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Generics provide type safety and reusability. Use T as a placeholder for any type.

variants.title: Level 2: Generic Classes (Pair)
variants.description: Create a generic Pair class that can hold two objects of potentially different types.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Multiple type parameters allow flexibility. Each type parameter can represent a different type.

variants.title: Level 3: Builder Pattern (Computer)
variants.description: Implement the Builder pattern for complex object construction.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Builder pattern separates construction from representation. Each builder method returns 'this' for chaining.

variants.title: Level 3: Observer Pattern (Newsletter)
variants.description: Implement observer pattern for notification system.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Observer pattern allows one-to-many dependencies. When subject changes, all observers are notified.

variants.title: Level 4: Thread-Safe Singleton (Logger)
variants.description: Create a thread-safe singleton logger with double-checked locking.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Thread safety requires careful synchronization. Double-checked locking optimizes performance while ensuring safety.

variants.title: Level 4: Command Pattern (Remote Control)
variants.description: Implement command pattern for a universal remote control.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Command pattern encapsulates requests as objects. Store commands for execution and undo operations.

variants.title: Level 5: Reflection and Meta-programming (Object Analyzer)
variants.description: Use reflection to analyze object properties and methods dynamically.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Reflection allows runtime inspection of classes. Use Class object to access metadata about fields and methods.

variants.title: Level 5: Custom Annotation Processor (Validator)
variants.description: Create custom annotations and processor for object validation.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Annotations provide metadata. Use reflection to check if fields have annotations and process their values.

variants.title: Level 1: Class Definition (Person)
variants.description: Learn the basic structure of a class by creating a Person class.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Classes contain fields (data) that describe what an object can store. Think about basic information a person would have.

variants.title: Level 1: Class Definition (Vehicle)
variants.description: Create a Vehicle class to understand class structure.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: A class is like a template. Define what attributes every vehicle should have.

variants.title: Level 2: Object Instantiation (Creating a Person)
variants.description: Learn how to create actual objects from your class blueprint.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use the 'new' keyword to create objects. Each object is a separate instance that can hold different data.

variants.title: Level 2: Object Instantiation (Creating Vehicles)
variants.description: Practice creating multiple objects from the same class.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Multiple objects can be created from the same class. Each will be independent of the others.

variants.title: Level 3: Accessing Object Fields (Person Properties)
variants.description: Learn how to set and access object properties using dot notation.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use dot notation (object.field) to access and modify object properties.

variants.title: Level 3: Accessing Object Fields (Product Details)
variants.description: Practice setting object fields with a Product class.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Objects can store different types of data. Use the appropriate data type for each field.

variants.title: Level 4: Simple Methods (Person Introduction)
variants.description: Add behavior to your class by creating methods.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Methods define what objects can do. Use the object's fields inside the method to create personalized output.

variants.title: Level 4: Simple Methods (Calculator Functions)
variants.description: Create a Calculator class with basic mathematical operations.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Methods can take parameters and return values. Consider what type of value each mathematical operation should return.

variants.title: Level 5: Method Parameters (Student Grade Calculator)
variants.description: Create methods that work with parameters to perform calculations.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Methods can process input parameters and return results. Think about what logic each method needs to perform.

variants.title: Level 5: Method Parameters (Circle Geometry)
variants.description: Create a Circle class with methods for geometric calculations.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Mathematical formulas can be implemented as methods. Use 3.14159 for π or Math.PI if you know it.

variants.title: Level 1: Constructors (Account Setup)
variants.description: Learn how constructors initialize objects when they're created.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Constructors ensure objects are properly initialized. They have the same name as the class and no return type.

variants.title: Level 1: Constructors (Employee Setup)
variants.description: Create an Employee class with proper initialization.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Constructors can take parameters to set initial values. Getters and setters provide controlled access to private fields.

variants.title: Level 2: Encapsulation (Student Records)
variants.description: Use private fields and public methods to protect data integrity.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Encapsulation protects data by validating inputs in setter methods. Don't allow invalid values to be stored.

variants.title: Level 2: Encapsulation (Temperature Converter)
variants.description: Create a temperature class that maintains valid temperature ranges.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Physical laws provide natural validation rules. Temperature cannot go below absolute zero.

variants.title: Level 3: Object Composition (Library System)
variants.description: Create classes that work together to model a library system.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Composition means objects contain other objects. A library contains books and members that interact.

variants.title: Level 3: Object Composition (Order System)
variants.description: Model an e-commerce order system with multiple related classes.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Real-world systems involve multiple objects working together. Focus on the relationships between entities.

variants.title: Level 4: Static Members (Utility Class)
variants.description: Create utility classes with static methods that don't require object instantiation.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Utility classes provide functionality without needing object state. Make constructor private and all methods static.

variants.title: Level 4: Static Members (ID Generator)
variants.description: Create a class that generates unique IDs using static fields and methods.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Static fields are shared across all instances. Perfect for generating unique IDs across the entire application.

variants.title: Level 5: Complex Object Interactions (Shopping Cart)
variants.description: Build a complete shopping cart system with multiple interacting classes.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Complex systems require careful design. Think about how items relate to the cart and what operations users need.

variants.title: Level 5: Complex Object Interactions (Grade Management)
variants.description: Create a comprehensive grade management system for a school.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Educational systems involve relationships between assignments, grades, and students. Model these carefully.

variants.title: Level 1: Advanced Design Patterns (Observer)
variants.description: Implement the Observer pattern for event notification system.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Observer pattern enables loose coupling. Objects can be notified of changes without tight dependencies.

variants.title: Level 1: Advanced Design Patterns (Strategy)
variants.description: Implement Strategy pattern for interchangeable algorithms.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Strategy pattern allows algorithms to be selected at runtime. Encapsulate each algorithm in its own class.

variants.title: Level 2: Generic Collection (Custom ArrayList)
variants.description: Create a generic dynamic array implementation with type safety.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Generic collections provide type safety. Handle array resizing when capacity is exceeded.

variants.title: Level 2: Generic Collection (Custom HashMap)
variants.description: Implement a basic hash map with generic key-value pairs.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Hash maps use arrays and linked lists for collision handling. Implement a simple hash function.

variants.title: Level 3: Decorator Pattern (Coffee Shop)
variants.description: Implement decorator pattern for a flexible coffee ordering system.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Decorator pattern adds behavior dynamically. Each decorator wraps the original object and adds features.

variants.title: Level 3: Factory Method Pattern (Shape Creator)
variants.description: Create a factory method pattern for shape object creation.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Factory method delegates object creation to subclasses. Each factory creates a specific type of object.

variants.title: Level 4: Thread-Safe Singleton (Connection Pool)
variants.description: Implement a thread-safe singleton connection pool with lazy initialization.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Thread-safe singleton requires careful synchronization. Use double-checked locking for performance.

variants.title: Level 4: Builder Pattern with Validation (Computer Builder)
variants.description: Create a builder pattern with validation for complex object construction.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Builder pattern with validation ensures objects are created in valid states. Validate required fields before building.

variants.title: Level 5: Reflection Framework (Object Mapper)
variants.description: Create an object mapping framework using reflection to convert between objects.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Reflection allows runtime inspection and manipulation of objects. Use Field and Method classes for dynamic access.

variants.title: Level 5: Annotation-Based Validation (Custom Validator)
variants.description: Create a validation framework using custom annotations and reflection.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Annotations provide metadata for validation rules. Use reflection to check field values against annotation constraints.
